serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of grape seeds is approximately 10 g, equivalent to the seeds extracted from a small bunch of grapes.

nutrient_source.title: Grape seeds, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 09132 / FDC ID 174683)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw grape seeds used directly. Hand-peeling is a mechanical separation step that does not alter the nutritional composition of the seed itself; values are therefore applied without adaptation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for isolated grape seeds in the University of Sydney GI Database. Grape seeds are predominantly composed of indigestible tannins, proanthocyanidins, and dietary fibre (12.4 g/100 g) with a low available carbohydrate fraction. A GI of 25 is estimated by analogy with other high-fibre, low-available-carbohydrate seeds and legumes (e.g. soya beans GI ~15–20, lentils GI ~29) from the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 10 g serving = (25 × 1.84 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.5. This is a conservative low-GI estimate; actual GI may be lower given the very high tannin and fibre content.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content and high potassium content
hypertension.guidance: Grape seeds contain only 10 mg sodium and 1089 mg potassium per 100 g, providing a highly favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio that supports blood pressure management; typical serving sizes are small (≈10 g), so absolute potassium contribution is modest but the food poses no hypertensive risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High dietary fibre and low available carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With 12.4 g dietary fibre and an estimated low glycemic index of approximately 25, grape seeds have a negligible glycemic load per serving (≈0.5) and are unlikely to cause postprandial blood glucose excursions; proanthocyanidins in grape seeds have also been associated with improved insulin sensitivity in preclinical studies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in proanthocyanidins and polyunsaturated fatty acids; zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grape seeds contain 6.1 g polyunsaturated fat, zero cholesterol, and are a concentrated source of oligomeric proanthocyanidins (OPCs), which have demonstrated antioxidant and endothelial-protective properties in peer-reviewed literature; the low saturated fat content (2.6 g/100 g) and high fibre content further support a cardioprotective dietary profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grape seeds (hand-peeled) are the small, hard kernels extracted manually from fresh grapes (Vitis vinifera). In the context of the field survey listing from Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles in Singapore, these are likely served as a novelty snack or functional food item, reflecting growing consumer interest in grape seed nutrition. The seeds are mechanically separated from the grape pulp by hand-peeling, leaving the raw seed intact. Raw grape seeds have a distinctly bitter, astringent taste due to their very high concentration of oligomeric proanthocyanidins (OPCs) and tannins. The texture is firm and slightly crunchy. Nutritionally, grape seeds are notable for their high dietary fibre content (approximately 12.4 g per 100 g), moderate protein (13.6 g), and a favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio. They are a concentrated source of polyphenolic antioxidants, particularly proanthocyanidins, which have been studied for cardiovascular, anti-inflammatory, and insulin-sensitising effects. The glycemic load per typical 10 g serving is negligible (approximately 0.5), making them suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Grape seeds are naturally free of cholesterol, gluten, and dairy, and are suitable for vegan and halal diets. They are not a common standalone food in traditional Chinese cuisine but are increasingly available as a functional snack in Singapore's health-conscious food market.
common_user_queries: What are grape seeds good for?
Are grape seeds safe to eat?
How many calories in grape seeds?
Are grape seeds high in fibre?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy for raw grape seeds, which is an approved primary source and represents an exact match to the food item. The GI value is an analogous estimate as no direct GI measurement for isolated grape seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; this introduces moderate uncertainty in the glycemic fields. The food identity is inferred from the field survey listing name 'Hand-peeled Grape Kernels' at a Lanzhou noodle restaurant, which is an unusual menu item; the canonical interpretation as raw grape seeds is the most defensible reading.
notes: 1. The input food_group was listed as 'Grains and staples', which is incorrect for grape seeds; reclassified to 'Snacks' / 'Edible seeds and kernels'. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as grape seeds are a plant food with no industrially produced trans fats; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. sugar_g is set to 0.0 as USDA SR Legacy reports negligible free sugars in grape seeds; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. vitamin_c_mg is set to 0.0 as USDA SR Legacy reports 0 mg vitamin C for grape seeds; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is set to 0.0 as USDA SR Legacy reports 0 mcg RAE for grape seeds; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is set to 0.0 as grape seeds are a plant food containing no vitamin B12; naturally zero. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is set to 0.0 as USDA SR Legacy reports 0 mcg vitamin D for grape seeds; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 8. cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0 as grape seeds are a plant food; naturally zero. 9. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for grape seeds. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for grape seeds. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for grape seeds. 12. GI is estimated at 25 by analogy with high-fibre low-available-carbohydrate seeds; no direct GI measurement exists for isolated grape seeds in the University of Sydney GI Database. 13. The restaurant context (Lanzhou beef noodle restaurant) is unusual for a grape seed product; the food is treated as a standalone snack item as listed. 14. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules as the food is served in Singapore by a Chinese cuisine restaurant.
